opp.name: HHMI_Spend+CLM+P2P+Intake+ANA_April'26
opp.next_step: 6/3/26 MQ Reaching out again to schedule discovery. Josh has been unresponsive. Attempting to re-engage. 4/22/26 MQ Reaching out to Josh again to schedule discovery. He has been OOO. Looking to schedule call before EOM. 4/8/26 MQ. Scheduling follow up meeting with Josh 4/23 or 4/24 for deeper dive discovery including Solution Consultant.
opp.ais_why:
• Opportunity scope explicitly bundles Spend, CLM, P2P, Intake, and ANA (Autonomous Negotiation Agent), signaling appetite for the full AI stack at Howard Hughes Medical Institute.
• AIS score sits at 6 and Marc Quessenberry is bringing a Solution Consultant into the next discovery for a deeper dive, suggesting genuine interest.
• Qualified stage with discovery follow-up actively being scheduled, though contact Josh has been OOO slowing momentum.

items.quote: No EB identified; Josh Galicki (Procurement Director) is the sole contact, no budget or approval discussion, no Contact Role of EB/Executive Sponsor.
items.source: Avoma intro call 2026-04-03; Salesforce OpportunityContactRole (zero buyer roles)

items.quote: No DM identified; no discussion of who makes final vendor selection decision.
Josh Galicki is passive/silent; no advocate driving the deal internally.
No business problem, cost, or ROI articulated; Josh expressed satisfaction with Ariba.
No evaluation framework, RFP, or must-have requirements documented.
Approval chain, timeline, and evaluation stages unknown.
Contracting, legal review, InfoSec, and procurement approval workflow unknown.
Single contact (Josh Galicki); zero buyer-side Contact Roles on opportunity, well below multi-thread bar.

metrics.narrative: No quantified business problem or ROI articulated. On the 2026-04-03 intro call Josh Galicki (Procurement Director) described HHMI's current state but expressed no dissatisfaction or cost/efficiency pain that would drive a platform replacement. When asked 'any issues you guys are having?', Josh replied he is 'new... learning the landscape' and deflected to requesting an overview of Zycus. The only operational detail shared was supplier onboarding via Apex and spend dashboards in Tableau, with no complaint. Josh characterized the incumbent Ariba as 'the Cadillac of procurement systems... you really can't argue with it', signaling satisfaction, not pain. No Salesforce field (Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c, Compelling_Event__c) carries a quantified metric. To establish Metrics, the deal team must re-engage Josh and run a pain-discovery session that surfaces a concrete operational or financial problem (cycle time, maverick spend %, compliance gaps, tailspend leakage) with current-state cost, and co-builds a business case showing quantified savings or efficiency gain from the proposed Zycus modules.

sources: Salesforce Next_Step__c (three unilateral rep outreach attempts, no buyer reply)
champion.narrative: No champion identified. Josh Galicki (Procurement Director) is the sole contact engaged, and he has shown no advocacy or urgency. On the intro call he was polite and curious but expressed satisfaction with the incumbent Ariba and stated he is 'new... learning the landscape', positioning himself as an explorer, not a change agent. Josh requested the deck to 'share with IT partners' and proposed a follow-up Q&A with a solution consultant, but did not advocate for Zycus, introduce other stakeholders, or express a burning need to replace the current stack. Post-call, Josh went silent: three rep outreach attempts (2026-04-08, 2026-04-22, 2026-06-03) received no reply, signaling low engagement or competing priorities. He has not provided access to the EB, DM, or any senior stakeholder. Salesforce X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c, Champion_Identified__c are empty. No Avoma call shows Josh pushing the deal forward internally or defending Zycus against Ariba. To develop a Champion, the deal team must (1) re-engage Josh and assess whether he can become a champion (does he have a problem Zycus solves, access to the EB, and willingness to advocate?), and (2) if Josh remains passive, ask him to introduce the deal team to a senior procurement or IT leader who owns the platform strategy and has the influence and urgency to drive a vendor evaluation forward.

competition.narrative: Competing against the incumbent SAP Ariba and the status quo (do-nothing). On the intro call Josh stated 'We use Ariba... since 2011... guided buying since 2017... Ariba is really the Cadillac of procurement systems... you really can't argue with it', signaling strong incumbent satisfaction. Josh also described a well-integrated current stack: Workday ERP, Apex supplier onboarding, Tableau spend dashboards, Claude AI pilots, Auditoria AP automation. The competitive wedge must overcome inertia and demonstrate that Zycus delivers material value Ariba/Workday cannot (agentic AI, unified code base, tailspend control, CLM, iRisk proactive monitoring). No other named vendor (Coupa, GEP, Ivalua) was mentioned, so this is primarily an incumbent-displacement and change-management battle. Salesforce Competitors__c is empty but the intro call definitively names Ariba as the in-place solution. How we win: (1) surface a pain Ariba does not solve (e.g. contract compliance gaps from Sharepoint, tailspend leakage, lack of proactive supplier risk monitoring), (2) quantify the cost of that pain, (3) run a live Merlin Intake and Anna negotiation agent POC to show agentic AI value Ariba cannot match, (4) build a champion who owns the pain and will advocate internally for change, and (5) de-risk the migration by showing seamless Workday integration and phased rollout (overlay Merlin Intake first, then replace Ariba P2P module-by-module).

identify_pain.narrative: No pain identified. On the intro call Josh Galicki described HHMI's current state in positive or neutral terms, with no operational or financial pain articulated. When asked 'any issues you guys are having?', Josh responded 'I'm new... learning the landscape' and requested a Zycus overview rather than naming a problem. Josh characterized the incumbent Ariba as 'the Cadillac of procurement systems... you really can't argue with it', signaling satisfaction. He mentioned HHMI uses Ariba (since 2011, guided buying since 2017), Workday ERP, Apex for supplier onboarding, Tableau dashboards for spend analytics, and is piloting Claude AI for anomaly detection and Auditoria on the AP side—indicating a well-tooled, mature procurement function with no clear gap. The only ambiguity was around CLM ('Do you have a CLM system?' Josh: 'No'... contracts reside in Sharepoint), but Josh did not express this as a pain point. Salesforce free-text fields Customer_Business_Problem__c, Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c, Compelling_Event__c, What_if_this_is_not_done__c are empty. To Identify Pain, the deal team must re-engage and run a discovery deep-dive that asks: what is broken or costly in the current Ariba/Workday/Sharepoint stack, what operational bottlenecks exist (maverick spend, slow cycle time, supplier risk visibility, contract compliance gaps), what does procurement leadership want to achieve in the next 12 months that the current toolset cannot deliver, and what is the cost (financial, reputational, compliance risk) of not solving it.

paper_process.narrative: Paper process unknown. No discussion of contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement approval workflow, InfoSec requirements, or implementation scoping occurred on the intro call. Josh did not mention MSA, SOW, or any internal approval or vendor onboarding steps beyond his intent to 'share with IT partners'. Salesforce Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c, Mandate__c are empty. No Current_Contract_Expiration__c date for the incumbent Ariba, so no renewal forcing function is known. No partner or systems integrator involvement mentioned. To surface the Paper Process, the deal team must ask Josh (or the EB once identified) to describe HHMI's procurement approval and contracting workflow: does a $250k SaaS purchase require board or CFO sign-off, what is the legal review and redline cycle, does InfoSec run a security questionnaire, how long does vendor onboarding take, and is an SI or implementation partner involved in the scoping or delivery.

sources: Account contacts (VP Strategic Procurement, VP/CFO not engaged)
economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer not identified. The intro call was with Josh Galicki, Procurement Director, who is new to HHMI (joined 'a couple months ago') and exploring vendor landscape but has not demonstrated budget authority or commercial decision-making power. No discussion of budget, pricing, or approval process occurred on the call. Josh stated he would 'share it with some of my IT partners' after reviewing the deck, suggesting he is an influencer gathering information, not the budget holder. No Salesforce Contact Role of Economic Buyer or Executive Sponsor exists. Salesforce free-text fields (X10a_Sponsor__c, Who_will_approve_budget__c, Purchase_approvals_Required_from__c) are empty. Account-level contacts include VP Strategic Procurement (a likely EB candidate) and VP/CFO Daniel Cronin, but neither has been engaged on this opportunity. The evidence shows only a single mid-level contact exploring, with no visibility to who controls the commercial decision or approves a $250k+ SaaS spend. To identify the EB, the deal team must ask Josh directly who approves procurement technology investments of this size, request an introduction to that executive, and validate budget authority and approval chain in a direct conversation.

decision_process.narrative: Limited process visibility. The intro call revealed a multi-stakeholder structure but no formal decision process. Josh stated he would 'share with some of my IT partners' and requested a follow-up 'question and answer session' with a Zycus solution consultant to address 'technical aspects', signaling IT involvement in the evaluation. HHMI procurement is centralized (21-person team, HQ in Chevy Chase, Maryland) with a strategic sourcing group, but Josh did not describe the approval chain, timeline, or how a vendor selection would proceed. No mention of RFP stages, finalist selection, or executive sign-off. Salesforce What_is_the_decision_process__c and Purchase_approvals_Required_from__c are empty. The call sequence itself is thin (one intro call, no subsequent meetings) so we cannot infer process from stakeholder escalation. To map the Decision Process, the deal team must ask Josh to describe the end-to-end evaluation and approval path: who participates at each stage (procurement, IT, finance, legal), who makes the final call, what gates exist (technical review, pricing, EB approval, contracting), and the expected timeline from vendor evaluation to contract signature.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria not documented. The intro call was a high-level Zycus capabilities overview with no discussion of HHMI's evaluation framework, selection criteria, or must-have requirements. Josh asked clarifying questions about delivery method (overlay vs standalone), AI usage, and integration with Ariba, but did not articulate what HHMI would evaluate vendors against (e.g. ease of adoption, AI maturity, CLM functionality, cost vs Ariba, implementation timeline). No RFP, RFI, or structured evaluation process was mentioned. Salesforce fields Business_Requirements__c, Top_Challenges_Priorities__c, Decision_Maker_Identified__c, Shoe_Fit_Criteria_Met__c are empty. To establish Decision Criteria, the deal team must re-engage and run a requirements workshop or BRD review session that surfaces HHMI's prioritized evaluation criteria, scoring model, and technical/functional must-haves for each module (eProcurement, CLM, Spend Analytics, Merlin Intake), and documents how Zycus will be measured against Ariba (incumbent) and any other alternatives.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI Curious. Josh stated HHMI is actively piloting AI (Claude for daily use, anomaly detection, spend analysis; Auditoria on AP side; exploring other tools), signaling openness and experimentation. However, no AI-driven pain or strategic AI roadmap was articulated, and Josh's focus was on understanding Zycus capabilities broadly, not AI specifically. The intro call shows curiosity but not hunger. To elevate to AI Hungry, the deal team must connect Merlin agentic AI to a concrete HHMI procurement priority (e.g. autonomous contract compliance, proactive supplier risk monitoring, tailspend reduction) and quantify the advantage over HHMI's current LLM pilots.

deal_movement.summary: Deal created 2026-04-03, qualified same day after intro call with Josh Galicki (Procurement Director). Single buyer touchpoint on 2026-04-03; Josh requested deck and proposed follow-up Q&A with solution consultant. Rep attempted to schedule follow-up on 2026-04-08 (for 2026-04-23/24), but no meeting occurred. Rep outreach on 2026-04-22 noted Josh 'has been OOO'. Rep outreach on 2026-06-03 noted Josh 'has been unresponsive'. Zero buyer engagement since intro call 83 days ago. No stage progression, no stakeholder expansion, no activity. Deal is cold.

Single point of contact with no multi-threading. If Josh deprioritizes the evaluation or leaves HHMI, the deal evaporates. Zero buyer-side Contact Roles means no organizational visibility or redundancy.

items.expected_effect: Validate the deal is real, funded, and has executive sponsorship, or disqualify. An exec-to-exec conversation with the VP Strategic Procurement can assess whether a platform evaluation is on HHMI's roadmap and surface the true EB and approval chain.

Josh requested follow-up Q&A, but went silent; three rep outreach attempts over 83 days received no reply. Zero multi-threading, no EB/DM visibility, no pain or business case, incumbent satisfaction high. All signals point to low priority or dead deal.

north_star_verdict.math: Close date 2027-04-30 is 308 days out. Created 2026-04-03 (84 days ago). Qualified stage entered same day. Time-in-Qualified: 84 days with no buyer engagement beyond the initial intro call. Last verified buyer engagement: 2026-04-03 (83 days ago, the intro call). Rep attempted re-engagement 2026-04-08, 2026-04-22, and 2026-06-03 with no buyer reply. Cold for 83 days forces Off Track regardless of stage.
evidence: Single buyer call on 2026-04-03 (intro call with Josh Galicki, Procurement Director). Josh stated he was new (joined 'a couple months ago'), exploring landscape, requested deck for internal review with IT partners.
Rep scheduled follow-up for 2026-04-23 or 2026-04-24 per call close; no evidence that meeting occurred.

Next Step history shows three unilateral rep outreach attempts: 2026-04-08 (scheduling follow-up), 2026-04-22 ('Josh has been OOO'), 2026-06-03 ('Josh has been unresponsive'). All are rep-initiated with no buyer reply.
Zero buyer engagement since 2026-04-03 intro call (83 days as of 2026-06-26). No completed Tasks or Events indicating two-way conversation.
Stage Qualified with $250k amount, close date 2027-04-30 (308 days out), and Pipeline forecast. No movement in 84 days signals deal is stalled at earliest qualification stage, not progressing.

Incumbent SAP Ariba identified on intro call ('We use Ariba... since 2011... Ariba is the Cadillac... you really can't argue with it'). No pain articulated that would drive replacement; buyer exploring but not committed to change.
north_star_verdict.headline: Deal remains cold for 83 days after single intro call on April 3. Buyer Josh Galicki unresponsive to three follow-up attempts. No identified pain, no two-way engagement, incumbent Ariba entrenched. Early-stage disqualification signal persists.

flags: Single point of contact (Josh Galicki, Procurement Director) with zero buyer-side Contact Roles on the opportunity. Multi-thread immediately to the relevant stakeholder (account contact) or IT leadership to build redundancy and access to decision-makers. A deal that depends on one mid-level contact who has gone silent for 83 days has no path to close.
best_practice_check.summary: Deal is cold and single-threaded after 83 days of buyer silence. Immediate priority: re-engage Josh or escalate to senior stakeholder to assess deal viability. If viable, run pain-discovery deep-dive to surface quantified business case that overcomes Ariba satisfaction and inertia. Multi-thread to VP Strategic Procurement or IT leadership to build champion and map EB/DM.

competitive_position.summary: Competing against incumbent SAP Ariba (in place since 2011, expanded to guided buying 2017) and the status quo. Ariba is the primary threat; Josh characterized it as 'the Cadillac of procurement systems... you really can't argue with it', signaling high satisfaction and inertia. HHMI's current stack is well-tooled (Ariba, Workday, Apex, Tableau, Claude AI pilots), so displacement requires surfacing a material pain Ariba does not solve and quantifying the cost. No other named competitor. This is an incumbent-retention and change-management battle, threat level high due to satisfaction and 83 days of buyer silence.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba (incumbent)
competitors.quote: We use Ariba... since 2011... guided buying since 2017... Ariba is really the Cadillac of procurement systems. I mean, where I came from before... we had a system called Prism, which is nowhere near the capabilities of Ariba. So, I mean, you really can't argue with it.
competitors.how_we_win: Surface a pain Ariba does not solve (e.g. contract compliance gaps from Sharepoint CLM, tailspend leakage, lack of proactive supplier risk monitoring via iRisk); quantify cost of pain; run live Merlin Intake + Anna POC to demonstrate agentic AI advantage Ariba cannot match; build champion who owns the pain; de-risk migration with phased overlay approach (Merlin Intake first, then module-by-module Ariba replacement).

competitors.name: Status quo / do-nothing
competitors.quote: Josh is new, 'learning the landscape', exploring but not committed. No pain articulated, no urgency, 83 days silent.
competitors.source: Avoma intro call 2026-04-03; Next_Step__c history (three unilateral rep outreach attempts, no buyer reply)
competitors.how_we_win: Create urgency by tying Zycus to a business outcome HHMI leadership cares about (cost savings, audit/compliance readiness, AI-driven efficiency); build a champion with influence; secure EB sponsorship; run ROI workshop quantifying the cost of inaction.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus positioned agentic AI (Merlin, early adopter since 2002, 32 patents) as a core differentiator on the intro call, and Josh confirmed HHMI is actively piloting AI (Claude for anomaly detection, Auditoria AP automation). The AI fit is promising, but positioning is incomplete because Josh did not articulate a specific AI use case or pain that Merlin would solve better than HHMI's current AI pilots. To strengthen AI positioning, the deal team must run a Merlin Intake or Anna negotiation agent live POC that shows agentic AI delivering a business outcome (e.g. autonomous tailspend control, contract compliance enforcement) HHMI's current LLM-based tools cannot match.

items.quote: When you were talking about finding information from punch outs... That makes me assume this would be a plug in to an existing procurement system, or is it its own procurement system on its own?

Josh: 'We're trying to use Claude on a daily basis... we're using it as an anomaly tool detection for spending... Auditoria on the AP side... looking into other tools as well.' Marc positioned Merlin agentic AI, early adopter since 2002, 32 patents.

Marc: 'We have 1,124 APIs that we expose... open book and open channel into our solution set from the outside... it integrates with your ERP.' Josh asked if Merlin Intake 'can be overlaid onto Ariba or it can be a stand alone product'; Marc confirmed both.
items.criterion: Integration with Workday ERP and existing Ariba

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Limited fit visibility. Josh asked clarifying questions on delivery method (overlay vs standalone), integration with Ariba, and AI usage, indicating exploratory interest but no defined requirements. He requested the deck for review with IT partners, signaling multi-stakeholder evaluation ahead, but no formal criteria or must-haves were articulated. Zycus positioned agentic AI, unified code base, and full S2P suite as differentiators, but Josh did not confirm these align with HHMI priorities. Fit cannot be assessed without a requirements workshop.

deltas.subject: Single point of contact with no multi-threading. If Josh deprioritizes the evaluation or leaves HHMI, the deal evaporates. Zero buyer-side Contact Roles means no organizational visibility or redundancy.

deltas.subject: Single point of contact (Josh Galicki, Procurement Director) with zero buyer-side Contact Roles on the opportunity. Multi-thread immediately to the relevant stakeholder (account contact) or IT leadership to build redundancy and access to decision-makers. A deal that depends on one mid-level contact who has gone silent for 83 days has no path to close.

deltas.to: No quantified pain or business case to displace incumbent Ariba, which Josh characterized as 'the Cadillac... you really can't argue with it'. Before any furthe
deltas.subject: No quantified pain or business case to displace incumbent Ariba, which Josh characterized as 'the Cadillac... you really can't argue with it'. Before any further technical demo or Q&A, run a pain-discovery workshop that surfaces a concrete operational or financial problem (contract compliance gaps, tailspend leakage, supplier risk blind spots, slow cycle time) and co-builds a quantified business case (cost of problem, ROI from Zycus solution, payback period). Without pain, there is no urgency to replace a well-entrenched incumbent.

deltas.to: Economic Buyer and Decision Maker not identified after 84 days in Qualified stage. Before investing further sales effort, the deal team must map the approval ch
deltas.subject: Economic Buyer and Decision Maker not identified after 84 days in Qualified stage. Before investing further sales effort, the deal team must map the approval chain and secure access to the EB (likely VP Strategic Procurement or VP/CFO). Ask Josh (or escalate around him) who controls the budget for procurement technology investments of this size, what the approval process is, and request an introduction to that executive to validate the opportunity is real and funded.

value.name: Josh Galicki
value.risk: New to HHMI (joined 'a couple months ago'), exploring landscape but not advocating for change. Expressed satisfaction with incumbent Ariba. Went silent after intro call; unresponsive to three rep outreach attempts over 83 days. Low engagement, high ghost risk.
value.title: Procurement Director

value.name: SAP Ariba (incumbent)
value.quote: We use Ariba... since 2011... guided buying since 2017... Ariba is really the Cadillac of procurement systems. I mean, where I came from before... we had a system called Prism, which is nowhere near the capabilities of Ariba. So, I mean, you really can't argue with it.
value.how_we_win: Surface a pain Ariba does not solve (e.g. contract compliance gaps from Sharepoint CLM, tailspend leakage, lack of proactive supplier risk monitoring via iRisk); quantify cost of pain; run live Merlin Intake + Anna POC to demonstrate agentic AI advantage Ariba cannot match; build champion who owns the pain; de-risk migration with phased overlay approach (Merlin Intake first, then module-by-module Ariba replacement).

value.name: Status quo / do-nothing
value.quote: Josh is new, 'learning the landscape', exploring but not committed. No pain articulated, no urgency, 83 days silent.
value.source: Avoma intro call 2026-04-03; Next_Step__c history (three unilateral rep outreach attempts, no buyer reply)
value.how_we_win: Create urgency by tying Zycus to a business outcome HHMI leadership cares about (cost savings, audit/compliance readiness, AI-driven efficiency); build a champion with influence; secure EB sponsorship; run ROI workshop quantifying the cost of inaction.

value.detail: No Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or Executive Sponsor identified or engaged after 84 days. Deal depends on a single mid-level contact (Josh Galicki, Procurement Director) who has gone silent. Without EB sponsorship and senior-level alignment, there is no approved budget, no urgency, and no path to a $250k commercial decision.
